description: Comprehensive E2E test coverage for browser tools, covering gaps in existing tool testing and full coverage for the new HAR export and WebSocket frame capture tools. Adds test server routes for WebSocket and HAR scenarios, and thorough test suites exercising happy paths, edge cases, error handling, and cross-tool interactions.

userStories.title: Add WebSocket test server endpoint for E2E tests
userStories.description: As a test author, I want a WebSocket endpoint on the E2E test server so that tests can exercise WebSocket frame capture against a real WebSocket connection.
acceptanceCriteria: test-server.ts creates a WebSocket server (using the ws package) alongside the HTTP server on the same port
WebSocket endpoint at /ws accepts connections and echoes received messages back with a 'echo: ' prefix
WebSocket endpoint sends an initial 'welcome' message upon connection
A new HTML page at /websocket-test is served that connects to ws://localhost:PORT/ws on page load and sends a test message
The /websocket-test page sends at least 3 messages ('hello', 'world', JSON.stringify({type:'ping'})) with 100ms delays between them
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes: Edit e2e/test-server.ts. The test server already uses node:http (line 33). Use the 'ws' package (WebSocketServer) to upgrade HTTP connections at path '/ws'. The ws package is already a dependency in the monorepo (used by the MCP server). Create the WebSocket server with { server, path: '/ws' }. On 'connection', send 'welcome', then on 'message' echo back with 'echo: ' prefix. Add a new route handler for GET /websocket-test that serves an HTML page. The page should create a WebSocket connection to `ws://${location.host}/ws`, and on open send three messages with setTimeout delays. The page should also display received messages in a <div id='ws-messages'> for verification. Make sure the WebSocket server is cleaned up in the server.close() handler.

userStories.title: Add HAR export test endpoint to test server
userStories.description: As a test author, I want a test page that generates diverse network traffic so that HAR export E2E tests can verify the exported HAR contains all request types.
acceptanceCriteria: A new HTML page at /har-test is served that triggers multiple types of network requests on page load
The page makes at least: one GET request (to /api/status-code/200), one POST request with JSON body (to /api/echo), and one request that returns a 404 (to /api/status-code/404)

The page shows a 'done' indicator (e.g.,
done
) after all requests complete
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes: Edit e2e/test-server.ts. Add a new route for GET /har-test. The page HTML should use fetch() to make the requests. Use Promise.all to wait for all requests, then set the #har-done element's text to 'done'. The /api/status-code/:code route already exists (it returns the requested status code). The /api/echo route already exists (POST, echoes the body). This gives us GET, POST with body, and error status codes to verify in the HAR output.

userStories.title: E2E tests for WebSocket frame capture
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want E2E tests that verify WebSocket frame capture works end-to-end so that we have confidence the feature works correctly through the full stack (MCP server -> extension -> CDP -> WebSocket).
acceptanceCriteria: New describe block 'Browser tools -- WebSocket frame capture' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: enable capture -> navigate to /websocket-test -> wait for frames -> get frames -> verify sent and received frames are captured with correct direction, data, and url fields
Test: get frames with clear=true empties the frame buffer and subsequent get returns empty array
Test: WebSocket frames respect urlFilter on enable_network_capture (filter that excludes /ws path captures no frames)
Test: browser_get_websocket_frames returns empty array when no WebSocket connections exist on the page
Test: browser_get_websocket_frames returns error for non-existent tabId
All tests pass reliably (no flaky timing issues) using polling pattern from existing waitForNetworkRequests helper
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes: Edit e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts. Add a new describe block after the existing 'Browser tools -- network body capture' block (around line 2424). Create a waitForWsFrames helper function following the pattern of waitForNetworkRequests (lines 129-152): poll browser_get_websocket_frames until at least N frames are captured, with 10s timeout and 300ms interval. The test flow: (1) open a tab to testServer /websocket-test, (2) enable network capture on that tab, (3) wait for frames using the helper (the page sends 3 messages and receives welcome + 3 echoes = 7 total frames expected, but just wait for >= 3 as a reliable threshold), (4) call browser_get_websocket_frames, (5) verify frames have url containing '/ws', direction is 'sent' or 'received', data is a string. For the clear test: get frames with clear=true, then get again and verify empty array. For urlFilter test: enable with urlFilter='/api' (won't match '/ws'), navigate to /websocket-test, wait briefly, verify 0 frames captured. Use the testServer fixture and mcpClient fixture already available in the test file.

userStories.title: E2E tests for HAR export
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want E2E tests that verify HAR export produces valid HAR 1.2 JSON so that we have confidence the export works correctly and produces standard-compliant output.
acceptanceCriteria: New describe block 'Browser tools -- HAR export' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: enable capture -> navigate to /har-test -> wait for requests -> export HAR -> verify it has valid HAR 1.2 structure (log.version === '1.2', log.creator.name exists, log.entries is an array)
Test: HAR entries contain the expected requests (GET 200, POST with body, 404 response)
Test: HAR entry request fields are correct (method, url includes the path, headers is array of {name,value})
Test: HAR entry response fields are correct (status is a number, content.mimeType exists)
Test: HAR entry with POST request includes postData.text containing the original request body
Test: HAR entry response includes content.text with the response body
Test: HAR entry queryString correctly parses URL query parameters when present
Test: browser_export_har with clear=true clears the request buffer
Test: browser_export_har returns error for non-existent tabId or tab without active capture
All tests pass reliably
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes: Edit e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts. Add a new describe block after the WebSocket tests. The test flow: (1) open tab to testServer /har-test, (2) enable network capture, (3) wait for the /har-test page to complete (use browser_wait_for_element with selector '#har-done'), (4) wait for network requests using waitForNetworkRequests helper, (5) call browser_export_har, (6) parse the returned HAR JSON and validate structure. The HAR should be returned as { har: string } where the string is JSON. Parse it and validate: har.log.version === '1.2', har.log.entries is an array with length >= 3, each entry has .request.method, .request.url, .request.headers (array), .response.status, .response.content. Find the POST entry and verify postData.text exists. Find the 404 entry and verify response.status === 404. For query string test: use a request to /api/status-code/200?foo=bar and verify queryString contains {name:'foo', value:'bar'}. The /har-test page should include a request with a query string parameter for this test.

userStories.title: E2E tests for network capture edge cases
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want E2E tests covering network capture edge cases (header redaction, maxRequests ring buffer, concurrent captures on different tabs, sensitive header handling) so that we have confidence in the robustness of the capture engine.
acceptanceCriteria: New describe block 'Browser tools -- network capture edge cases' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: captured requests have Authorization headers redacted to 'Bearer [REDACTED]' format (preserves scheme)
Test: captured requests have Cookie headers redacted to '[REDACTED]'
Test: maxRequests parameter limits buffer size (enable with maxRequests=2, generate >2 requests, verify only 2 returned)
Test: concurrent network captures on two different tabs are independent (enable on tab A and tab B, generate different traffic on each, verify each tab's requests are isolated)
Test: disabling capture on one tab does not affect capture on another tab
Test: network capture survives page navigation (enable capture, navigate to new page, verify new page's requests are captured)
Test: failed requests (status 0) are captured with error text
All tests pass reliably
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes: Edit e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts. Add a new describe block. For the header redaction test: the test server /api/echo endpoint is called from the test page. Use browser_execute_script to inject a fetch call that includes an Authorization header: `fetch('/api/echo', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Authorization': 'Bearer my-secret-token', 'Cookie': 'session=abc123' }, body: JSON.stringify({msg:'test'}) })`. Then get network requests and verify the Authorization header value is 'Bearer [REDACTED]' and Cookie is '[REDACTED]'. For maxRequests: enable with maxRequests=2, use execute_script to trigger 5 rapid fetch calls to different endpoints, poll until stable, verify requests.length <= 2. For concurrent captures: open two tabs, enable capture on both, execute_script on each to trigger different requests, verify each tab's requests are independent. For failed requests: use the existing /api/slow-forever endpoint with a very short timeout or fetch a non-existent host — look at how the test server works. The /api/status-code/:code route can return various codes. For a failed request, try fetching an unreachable host from the page (e.g., fetch('http://192.0.2.1/timeout', { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(100) }).catch(() => {})).

userStories.title: E2E tests for console log capture edge cases
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want E2E tests covering console log capture edge cases (level filtering, maxConsoleLogs limit, various console methods) so that we have confidence in the robustness of console capture.
acceptanceCriteria: New describe block 'Browser tools -- console log edge cases' in browser-tools.e2e.ts
Test: console.warn messages are captured with level 'warning'
Test: console.info messages are captured with level 'info'
Test: console.debug messages are captured with level 'debug'
Test: level filter on browser_get_console_logs returns only matching entries (e.g., level='error' filters out log/warn/info)
Test: console.log with multiple arguments joins them with spaces
Test: console.log with object argument includes object description
All tests pass reliably
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
npm run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes: Edit e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts. Add a new describe block after the existing console log tests (around line 1817). For each test: enable network capture (required for console capture to work), then use browser_execute_script to trigger the console method (e.g., `console.warn('test-warn-message')`), then poll browser_get_console_logs until the message appears. Verify the level field matches CDP's level names: 'warning' for console.warn, 'info' for console.info, 'debug' for console.debug, 'log' for console.log, 'error' for console.error. For the filter test: produce messages at multiple levels, then call get_console_logs with level='error' and verify only error messages are returned. For multi-argument test: execute `console.log('hello', 'world', 42)` and verify message is 'hello world 42'. For object argument: execute `console.log({key: 'value'})` and verify the message contains some representation of the object. Use the existing waitForConsoleLogs helper.
